flashcards.front: Why is __syncthreads() unnecessary for the last 5 iterations of tree reduction in a 256-thread block?
flashcards.back: When the stride drops to 32 or below, all remaining active threads (0-31) belong to warp 0. Threads within a warp execute in lockstep (SIMT model), so they are implicitly synchronized and do not need an explicit barrier.

It is a warp shuffle instruction where thread i receives the register value of 'val' from thread i + delta within the same warp. The mask 0xFFFFFFFF indicates all 32 threads participate. It transfers data directly between registers without using shared memory, at ~1 cycle latency.

flashcards.front: Describe the two-phase reduction for multi-warp blocks using warp shuffles.
flashcards.back: Phase 1: Each warp independently reduces its 32 values to a single value using __shfl_down_sync() (5 shuffle operations, no shared memory). Lane 0 of each warp writes the result to a shared memory array. Phase 2: After one __syncthreads(), warp 0 loads the per-warp sums and performs another warp shuffle reduction to produce the final block sum.

flashcards.front: Why is the volatile keyword needed for shared memory in pre-Volta warp-level reduction code?
flashcards.back: Without __syncthreads(), the compiler may optimize shared memory reads by caching values in registers. Declaring the shared memory pointer as volatile forces every read/write to go directly to shared memory, ensuring that threads within the warp see each other's most recent writes. On Volta+ (CC 7.0+), use __shfl_down_sync() instead.

flashcards.front: How does the two-phase warp-shuffle reduction improve performance over the full tree reduction?
flashcards.back: It reduces __syncthreads() calls from log2(blockDim.x) to just 1, uses warp shuffles (~1 cycle) instead of shared memory reads (~5 cycles), eliminates shared memory bank conflicts during intra-warp reduction, and reduces shared memory usage from blockDim.x entries to just 32 entries (one per warp), enabling higher occupancy.
